00055| SEMICONDUCTOR DIODE
00056| 1.1
00057| 
00058| DIODE
00059| In diode, two type of semiconductor materials are used
00060| 1.
00061| 
00062| n-type and
00063| 
00064| 2. p-type
00065| An n-type semiconductor has electrons as the majority carrier, and
00066| a p-type semiconductor has holes as the majority carrier.
00067| 
00068| Construction:
00069| •
00070| 
00071| Anode
00072| Metal
00073| contact
00074| 
00075| A semiconductor diode is formed simply by joining p-type and n-type
00076| semiconductor material.
00077| p-type
00078| material
00079| 
00080| n-type
00081| material
00082| 
00083| Cathode
00084| 
00085| Fig. 1.1.1 (a): Diode Structure
00086| 
00087| •
00088| 
00089| Anode
00090| 
00091| Cathode
00092| 
00093| Fig. 1.1.1 (b): Symbol
00094| 
00095| A PN junction diode is a 2 terminal, 2 layer, single junction
00096| semiconductor device made out of either Silicon or Germanium, with
00097| one side p-type & other side with n-type semiconductor & it is as
00098| shown in the figure 1.1.1(a).
00099| 1
00100| 
00101|  2
00102| 
00103| Basic Electronics
00104| 
00105| •
00106| 
00107| A diode is a unidirectional device because it allows the current to
00108| flow in only one direction (anode to cathode).
00109| 
00110| •
00111| 
00112| In a diode symbol, the arrowhead indicates the direction of current
00113| flowing through the diode.
00114| 
00115| •
00116| 
00117| PN Junction: When p-type and n-type materials are placed in
00118| contact with each other, the junction is called PN junction.
00119| 
00120| 1.1.1
00121| 
00122| Diffusion and Depletion region
00123| 
00124| •
00125| 
00126| When the two types of semiconductor materials are joined together, the
00127| electrons from the n-type material diffuse into p-type material and
00128| combines with holes. This creates a layer of negative ions near the
00129| junction in p-type material.
00130| 
00131| •
00132| 
00133| Similarly, the holes from the p-type material diffuse into n-type
00134| material resulting in a layer of positive ions in the n-type material.
00135| Diffusion
00136| 
00137| Depletion Layer
00138| 
00139| N
00140| 
00141| P
00142| 
00143| N
00144| 
00145| Fig.1.1.2 (a): Situation during diffusion Fig. 1.1.2(b):Situation after formation
00146| of depletion region
00147| 
00148| •
00149| 
00150| The figure 1.1.2(b) shows the situation after the formation of
00151| depletion region.
00152| 
00153| •
00154| 
00155| These two layers of positive and negative ions form the depletion
00156| region. The term “depletion” refers to the fact that the region near
00157| the junction is depleted of their respective majority charge
00158| carriers.
00159| 
00160| •
00161| 
00162| The depletion region consists of positive and negative ions on the
00163| opposite side of the junction. The electric field acts as a “barrier” which
00164| prevents further diffusion of electrons and holes after equilibrium is
00165| established.
00166| 
00167| •
00168| 
00169| So if we want to move an electron from n-type material to p-type
00170| material, energy must be supplied to the electrons to overcome the
00171| “barrier”. The external voltage required to move the electrons
00172| 
00173|  Semiconductor Diodes & Applications
00174| 
00175| 3
00176| 
00177| through the electric field is called barrier potential. Barrier
00178| potential is measured in volts.
00179| •
00180| 
00181| Typical value of barrier potential at 25C is 0.7 V for silicon
00182| and 0.3 V for germanium diode.
00183| 
00184| Note:
00185| •
00186| 
00187| Figure 1.1.2( c) gives the explanation after equilibrium is
00188| established.
00189| These holes will experience repulsive force from these positive ions
00190| which prevents further diffusion on holes in n-type material
00191| 
00192| Repulsive force due to these negative ions will be prevent these
00193| electrons from further diffusing in to p-type material
00194| Fig.1.1.2 (c): Situation after equilibrium
00195| 
00196| Unbiased PN Junction diode
00197| 
00198| N
00199| 
00200| Fig.1.1.3: Unbiased PN Junction diode
00201| •
00202| 
00203| 1.2
00204| 
00205| A diode without any external voltage is called unbiased.
00206| 
00207| BIASING
00208| •
00209| 
00210| Biasing refers to the application of external voltage across the two
00211| terminals of the diode.
00212| 
00213| •
00214| 
00215| Diode biasing can be done in two different ways
00216| 
00217|  4
00218| 
00219| Basic Electronics
00220| 
00221| 1. Forward biasing
00222| 2. Reverse biasing
00223| 
00224| 1.2.1
00225| 
00226| 
00227| Forward biasing
00228| Explain the operation of pn junction diode under forward and
00229| reverse bias condition.
00230| 
00231| When the positive terminal of the battery is connected to the p-type material
00232| and the negative terminal of the battery is connected to the n-type material, such
00233| a connection is called forward biasing.
00234| 
00235| N
00236| IF
00237| +
00238| VF
00239| Fig. 1.2.1: Forward biased p-n junction
00240| 
00241| •
00242| 
00243| The electrons and holes will cross the p-n junction as a result of reduced
00244| electric field in the depletion region.
00245| 
00246| •
00247| 
00248| Electrons from the n-region crosses the junction and enters the pregion. Since the positive terminal of the battery is connected to the
00249| p-region, the electrons experience an attractive force and moves to
00250| the positive terminal of the battery.
00251| 
00252| •
00253| 
00254| Similarly, holes from the p-region crosses the junction and enters the
00255| n-region. Since the negative terminal of the battery is connected to
00256| the n-region, the holes experience an attractive force and moves to
00257| the negative terminal of the battery.
00258| 
00259| •
00260| 
00261| With the reduction in net electric field, the holes can now flow into the
00262| n-region. As the holes pass through the depletion region, the number of
00263| negative ions also decreases. Hence the width of depletion region
00264| decreases due to reduction in the number of positive and negative ions.
00265| 
00266| •
00267| 
00268| The amount of energy required by the electrons to cross the
00269| junction is equal to the barrier potential (0.3 V for Ge and
00270| 0.7 V for Si).
00271| 
00272|  Semiconductor Diodes & Applications
00273| 
00274| 5
00275| 
00276| 1.2.2 Reverse biasing
00277| When the positive terminal of the battery is connected to n-type material
00278| and the negative terminal of the battery is connected to p-type material, such
00279| a connection is called reverse bias.
00280| 
00281| N
00282| IR
00283| +
00284| VR
00285| Fig. 1.2.2: Reverse biased p-n junction
00286| 
00287| •
00288| 
00289| When the diode is reverse biased, there is no flow of current due to
00290| majority carriers. However there is a very little flow of current (in nano
00291| ampere range for silicon diode) due to minority carriers.
00292| 
00293| •
00294| 
00295| In reverse biased, the electrons from the p-type semiconductor are
00296| pushed towards the p-n junction by the negative terminal of the battery.
00297| 
00298| •
00299| 
00300| Similarly, the holes from the n-type semiconductor are pushed towards
00301| the junction by the positive terminal of the battery.
00302| 
00303| •
00304| 
00305| This movement of electrons and holes constitutes a current called
00306| reverse saturation current. The term saturation refers to the fact
00307| that it reaches its maximum value very quickly and does not
00308| change significantly with increase in reverse bias potential.
00309| 
00310| •
00311| 
00312| The magnitude of reverse current is of the order of nano-amperes for
00313| silicon diodes.
00314| 
00315| •
00316| 
00317| However, when the reverse bias is increased beyond a certain limit, the
00318| reverse current increases drastically. The voltage beyond which the reverse
00319| current increases drastically is called reverse breakdown voltage.
00320| 
00321| 1.2.3 Diode V-I Characteristics
00322| 1.
00323| 
00324| 
00325| 
00326| 
00327| Silicon diode Forward & Reverse characteristics (Si):
00328| 
00329| Draw & explain the V-I characteristics of a silicon PN junction
00330| diode.
00331| Explain the forward and reverse characteristics of the diode.
00332| 
00333|  6
00334| 
00335| Basic Electronics
00336| 
00337| Forward characteristics:
00338| •
00339| 
00340| In forward characteristics of p-n junction diode, VF represents the
00341| forward voltage whereas IF represents the forward current.
00342| 
00343| •
00344| 
00345| Initially when the forward bias voltage is zero, the current through
00346| the diode is also zero.
00347| IF(mA)
00348| 
00349| VR volts
00350| 
00351| VFvolts
00352| 
00353| IR(A)
00354| Fig. 1.2.3: Forward & Reverse characteristics (V-I characteristics)
00355| of silicon diode
00356| •
00357| 
00358| If the external voltage applied on the silicon diode (Si) is less than
00359| 0.7 volts, the silicon diode allows only a small current (µA). However,
00360| this small current is considered as negligible.
00361| 
00362| •
00363| 
00364| When the external voltage applied on the silicon diode reaches
00365| 0.7 volts, the p-n junction diode starts allowing large current (mA)
00366| through it. At this point, a small increase in voltage increases the
00367| current rapidly.
00368| 
00369| •
00370| 
00371| The forward voltage at which the silicon diode starts allowing large
00372| current is called cut-in voltage or knee voltage. The cut-in voltage for
00373| silicon diode is approximately 0.7 volts.
00374| 
00375| Reverse characteristics:
00376| •
00377| •
00378| 
00379| In reverse characteristics of p-n junction diode, V R represents the
00380| reverse voltage whereas IR represents the reverse current.
00381| IR is due to minority charge carriers and is called reverse saturation
00382| current.
00383| 
00384|  Semiconductor Diodes & Applications
00385| 
00386| 7
00387| 
00388| •
00389| 
00390| The reverse current is low (3A) till breakdown is reached and hence the
00391| diode looks like an open circuit.
00392| 
00393| •
00394| 
00395| When a reversed-bias voltage is increased to a large value (breakdown
00396| voltage), a large reverse current will flow and diode goes into a
00397| reverse breakdown by which diode may get destroyed.
00398| 
00399| •
00400| 
00401| Reverse breakdown occurs at 75 V as shown in the figure 1.2.3.
00402| 
00403|   Semiconductor Diodes & Applications
00404|

00407| 1.5 RECTIFIERS
00408| Rectifier
00409| Rectifier is an electronic device that converts ac (alternating current) into
00410| dc (direct current). Semiconductor diodes (PN junction diodes) are used as
00411| rectifying elements.
00412| 
00413| Rectification
00414| Rectification is the process of converting alternating current (ac) into direct
00415| current (dc).
00416| Rectifiers are classified into
00417| 1.
00418| 
00419| Half wave rectifier (HWR)
00420| 
00421| 2.
00422| 
00423| Full wave rectifier (FWR)
00424| 
00425|  22
00426| 
00427| Basic Electronics
00428| 
00429| Full wave rectifier can be built up in 2 ways:
00430| 1.
00431| 
00432| Full – wave rectifier using 2 diodes & a centre tapped transformer
00433| (FWR).
00434| 
00435| 2.
00436| 
00437| Full – wave bridge rectifier using 4 diodes & an ordinary transformer
00438| (FWBR).

00622| Advantages of HWR:

01490| 1.5.2 Full wave rectifier with center-tapped transformer
01491| A center-tap full wave rectifier uses only two diodes and are connected to
01492| the opposite ends of a center-tapped secondary transformer as shown in fig.
01493| 1.5.2 (a). The center-tap ‘C’ is usually considered as the ground point
01494| (or the zero voltage reference point).

01617| Thus, the diode D2 conducts and the current flows through D2, the load

01628| 37
01629| 
01630| •
01631| 
01632| The current flowing through the load resistor RL is in the same direction
01633| during both the half cycle of the ac input. Hence, for both the half cycles,
01634| DC output voltage ‘Vo’ is obtained across the load resistor.
01635| 
01636| •
01637| 
01638| Thus, we get two half cycles for one complete input signal and hence, the
01639| name full wave rectifier.
01640| 
01641| Advantages of FWR:
01642| 1.
01643| 
01644| The efficiency of FWR is 81.2% (i.e. twice the efficiency of HWR).
01645| 
01646| 2.
01647| 
01648| The ripple factor is 0.48, which is much less than that of HWR
01649| 
01650| 3.
01651| 
01652| The larger DC output power.
01653| 
01654|  38
01655| 
01656| Basic Electronics
01657| 
01658| Disadvantages of FWR:
01659| 1.
01660| 2.
01661| 
01662| PIV rating of the diode is higher i.e. PIV = 2Vm
01663| 
01664| 3.
01665| 
01666| Center-tap transformer is costly.
01667| 
01668| 4.
01669| 
01670| Cost of the FWR is more than HWR.
01671| 
01672| It is difficult to locate the center on the secondary for the tapping. (Each
01673| diode utilizes only one-half of the voltage developed in the
01674| transformer secondary and thus the DC output obtained is small).
01675|
